---
output: github_document
---

<!-- README.md is generated from README.Rmd. Please edit that file -->

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.path = "man/figures/README-",
  out.width = "100%"
)
```

# dbtr

<!-- badges: start -->
<!-- badges: end -->

The goal of dbtr is to provide a collection of R tools for working with data generated by diabetes technologies including CGM sensors and pumps. Primarily developed for research rather than individual use. Simplifies API process for extracting data from Nightscout and creating summary metrics.
`dbtr` makes use of the `httr2` package for creating API calls and retrieving data, the `data.table` package for fast data cleaning, and the excellent `iglu` package for creating CGM summary metrics. 


## Installation

You can install the development version of dbtr from [GitHub](https://github.com/) with:

``` r
# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("jwilliman/dbtr")
```

## Example

This is a basic example to show how to extract treatment information from Nightscout and calculate daily insulin metrics, similar to what is displayed on the Nightscout dashboard.

```{r example, eval = FALSE}

library(dbtr)

# Extract treatment data from Nightscout

treatments <- ns_api_fetch(
  hostname   = "localhost:1337",
  filename   = "treatments", filetype = "json", 
  count      = Inf, 
  start_date = "2024-04-20",
  end_date   = "2024-05-04",
  api_secret = "nightscout_secret",
  perform    = TRUE) |> 
  httr2::resp_body_json() |> 
  data.table::rbindlist(fill = TRUE)

# Clean treatment data, creating local date variables
ns_clean_treatments(treatments, tz = "Pacific/Auckland")

# Create dataset with insulin delivery data
ns_insulin(treatments)


```

<!-- What is special about using `README.Rmd` instead of just `README.md`? You can include R chunks like so: -->

```{r cars, eval = FALSE, echo = FALSE}
summary(cars)
```

<!-- You'll still need to render `README.Rmd` regularly, to keep `README.md` up-to-date. `devtools::build_readme()` is handy for this. -->

<!-- You can also embed plots, for example: -->

```{r pressure, echo = FALSE, eval = FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

<!-- In that case, don't forget to commit and push the resulting figure files, so they display on GitHub and CRAN. -->
